replies.author: @KB024
replies.text:
The fact that you think this isn't further demonstration of subversion is testament to how doomed we truly are.

These stories suck. 
They're ugly nasty expressions of Martin's struggle with attempting to subvert epic fantasy, failing, realizing his failure and refusing to finish, refusing to get to the inevitable point of implosion which he knows awaits him at the end of ASOIAF, and instead trying to escape into a more-pleasant version of his subversive fantasy, hoping rubes won't realize that these stories *are still quite subversive* in various ways, first and foremost in that they're seemingly legitimately aimed at subverting Martin's own work.

tl;dr Martin: "I wanna subvert high-fantasy! Screw Tolkein!... Oh no, subverting high-fantasy completely guts the genre! And I can't turn back now!... Uh, uh, uh.... I know! I'll write a less-subversive high-fantasy story in the traditional fashion, and hope nobody notices I never finished the original work! Thats the ticket!"
Pathetic. He's never gonna finish, because he knows as well as I do that what he set out to do, the ending of it, doesn't please anyone, will lead to him being remembered as a perverted hack who became a - the - demonstration that attempting to be a nihilistic subversive pervert in the context of high-fantasy fundamentally doesn't work. The Dunk/Egg stories are just desperate attempts to escape the endpoint he set for himself.

I hope that's not what's going on here. For almost 2 decades, media has been plagued by the obsession with subversion. It's time people realise that subversion itself is not clever, interesting or innovative. Much like breaking the 4th wall in Deadpool, it's not impressive or intelligent to break a rule that's there for a reason, the real genius is knowing how to use the established narrative devices that work, to create something engaging, fun, sincere etc.

Doing something unexpected is, in itself, a tragic motivation when it comes to storytelling.

replies.text:
No, no, no! You rube! You simpleton! You damnable Dunk!

The ENTIRE POINT of the Dunk/Egg stories is that they're a cope. 

Martin tried to subvert traditional high-fantasy, to subvert Tolkein, he has expressed as much point-blank; his problem, which he didn't realize - dimwit sleaze that he is - until it was too late, is that attempting to approach high-fantasy as a genre, while being a perverted degenerate old pinko nihilist, fundamentally does not work. 
It guts the entire appeal of the genre, leaving you with a story that is both silly and childish as well as disappointingly depressing.

Dunk/Egg stories are his desperate attempt to escape that paradigm, to avoid having to reach the nihilistic depressing end-point this hyper-woke hack set for himself decades ago, and to achieve that what does he do? He reverts back to a - still extremely subversive imho - pseudo-traditional archetype. 
The very 'wholesomeness' you claim to see, to experience, is LITERALLY a conscious injection of such on behalf of Martin into this ugly nihilistic nightmare world he sought to create - its what he's holding up to distract you from noticing that he has yet to complete his magnum opus, which he cannot finish (because it will reveal him for the dull hack he always was) and which, if left unfinished, really paints the Dunk/Egg stories as complete wastes of time on behalf of both the reader and the author.

I'm sorry, but you folks really need to get your acts together. You're so desperate for ANY CRUMB of decency, you'll drop to your knees and fellate the first person that holds a door for you, even if they were only doing it to spite you.

replies.author: @Valterius87
replies.text: @1GOBIRDS1 Except for the fact that the a not insignificant amount of actual battles could last for hours, even days. If neither side routed, and initiative was there, armies could go at it for days(yes, resting at night but the day's battle could easily go on from sun up to sun down). Again, there is a plethora of content just right here on YT talking about this. The Romans even had a term for this: "Down to the Triarii". Modern UFC is hardly actual combat. The training and conditioning is absolutely different. They train for explosive strength and technical skill to end the fight quickly(as quickly as possible). Soldiers do the opposite, even in antiquity. They were absolutely trained for conditioning and prolonged exposure and exertion. Huge difference.

replies.author: @raics101
replies.text:
​​@donwyoming1936Modern day produced Buhurt equipment is usually heavier than what people would use back in the day. And it's for obvious reasons, it's a controlled environment so you won't die if you gas out, and you really don't want to report on your job tomorrow with a broken finger. Even in that, trained people can fight for a fairly long time, but real equipment would compromise some protection of less important parts for lower weight and better mobility.

In a way, it was also done back in the day. Tournament armor, if you can afford a second set, was heavier and often very focused on a specific role, like jousting, and wouldn't be very usable on battlefield.

replies.author: @channell11
replies.text: Sometimes simple is best. A lot of stories get so fixated on making the scope and setting as widespread and grand as possible that character development and richness of the plot get sidelined. The reason the first LOTR trilogy worked so well was that despite it being a fantasy epic with a grand scale, it still focused on the hero's journey-those good, noble role models with matching intentions. I think GRRM created a wonderful universe, but GoT and HoTD focused a lot more on palace intrigue, with few redeemable characters to root for. It was more like a soap opera, where you stay tuned to see what twist or betrayal happens next.

replies.text:
@hzozuppu0pu Yes! Why the hell not!
The same company could do it as the one that did this!
The money wouldn't be that much, this season was done on a comparatively-low budget for every episode, and if you're willing to put the risk in for a disappointing 3 hour borefest that everyone will forget before the next installment - and you can keep telling me all day how it'll be out this year, sure thing chief - then the risk for a 3x larger output and for a significantly higher cultural impact? Easily worth it.

If you're making excuses for crappy behavior on the basis of capitalism, then you're basically admitting outright the industry is a trashfest grounded purely in shekels, and I don't have any need to argue with on the basis of quality, because you have already abandoned any notion of expectation of quality, justifying it on the grounds of "well they need to serve the shareholders..."

10-20 episodes, 1 season, could cover ALL the extant Dunk and Egg content. It could easily have been done, for less cost than, say, a single season of House of the Dragon, with no greater risk to it.
Furthermore, it would have set the scene and provision time for Martin and the showrunners to start working on NEW Dunk and Egg material, perhaps oriented around the later part of Eggs life when he becomes King, which he would age into in a few years.
Instead, we're gonna draw this out for like 5 years and the Egg character is gonna be like 18 trying to play a 12 year old, and its gonna suck, and you know it.

You are making excuses for people who are bending you over. Stop it. Simple as.

text: Watched your review a few days ago, having never heard of the series.  I've just finished the sixth episode and man were you spot on!  THE best series that I've watched in a long time!  I watched the entire first season of GoT hoping that I would get hooked in like a lot of my friends.  Long story short, I disliked most of the characters, intensely.  Never watched another episode.  A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms is NOTHING like the first season of GoT, and for me that's a very good thing.  This series has characters that I actually LIKE and can root for.  Characters that display loyalty, honor, compassion, integrity and all of the other qualities that a person should strive for, without being trite or preachy.  Male characters who aren't weak, foolish or just pure evil.  Female characters who don't have to girl boss or Mary Sue their way through the story line, and who are both capable and likeable.  The only downside to the first season is that now I have wait far too long for a second season!

author: @cdecoro
text: Prince Baelor is a great example of how good writing and good acting can build a strong emotional connection in a very short period of time.  He has less than 10 minutes of screen time in the show, basically in four scenes (when Dunk first meets him in the castle to vouch for him, when he meets Dunk after his arrest to encourage trial by combat, when Dunk is brought before the Targaryans to demand his trial by combat, and after the trial when he dies).  And yet his death is profoundly felt by the viewer.  In just a few minutes in his first scene, we recognize that this is a leader with authority, but an authority that comes from the strength of his character, rather than threats or titles, who men would gladly follow and die for.

replies.author: @l.sophia2803
replies.text: @chrismcdonald7086 I think the problem is the type of women screwing it up. Im Gen X and we loved all the best Heroes Journey stories that the boys did, like the original Star Wars, etc. I remember asking a teacher in grade school why we used the term 'man' for things like business man. She said that in those cases 'man' includes women. I have never had a reason to argue with that. Mankind is obviously all of us in it together..its such a time waster thinking about this garbage of 'imbalance'. Joseph Campbell addressed the issue of women in these stories, like Lord of The Rings. He said that women ARE the crown, they are the grail that the men are claiming after proving worthiness to themselves. This is an epic point imo.

author: @ebonyknight1
text: I avoided this show since my late husband was a bit GoT fan [books] and we both felt so let down by the final season. Then he got to see the first season on HotD, which was fantastic, and redeemed the franchise in his mind...he died before Season 2, and I never was able to watch that without him.  He knew the Dunc / Egg story was being planned, its one of his favourite, so I obviously avoided it...but now, since this review I actually feel if it really is positive and uplifting, that he would've loved it too.  He was very much one for character driven tv with good writing. So thanks for bringing this show back to my attention. Maybe I'll get that Sky subscription after-all.
